Rotator cuff tears
Asheesh Bedi 1,2 , Julie Bishop 3 , Jay Keener 4 , Drew A. Lansdown 5 , Ofer Levy 6 , Peter MacDonald 7 , Nicola Maffulli
Joo Han Oh 9 , Vani J. Sabesan 10 , Joaquin Sanchez-Sotelo 11 , Riley J. Williams III 12  & Brian T. Feeley
Abstract
Sections

Rotator cuff tears are the most common upper extremity condition seen by primary care and orthopaedic surgeons, with a spectrum ranging from tendinopathy to full-thickness tears with arthritic change. Some tears are traumatic, but most rotator cuff problems are degenerative. Not all tears are symptomatic and not all progress, and many patients in whom tears become more extensive do not experience symptom worsening. Hence, a standard algorithm for managing patients is challenging. The pathophysiology of rotator cuff tears is complex and encompasses an interplay between the tendon, bone and muscle. Rotator cuff tears begin as degenerative changes within the tendon, with matrix disorganization and inflammatory changes. Subsequently, tears progress to partial-thickness and then full-thickness tears. Muscle quality, as evidenced by the overall size of the muscle and intramuscular fatty infiltration, also influences symptoms, tear progression and the outcomes of surgery. Treatment depends primarily on symptoms, with non-operative management sufficient for most patients with rotator cuff problems. Modern arthroscopic repair techniques have improved recovery, but outcomes are still limited by a lack of understanding of how to improve tendon to bone healing in many patients.

Introduction
Epidemiology
Prevalence

The rotator cuff is a group of muscles and tendons that maintains the shoulder in place and provides dynamic motion and stability to the joint. It is composed of four separate muscles, namely the supraspi- natus, infraspinatus, subscapularis and teres minor. The tendons from these four muscles coalesce at their attachments on the humeral head (Fig.  1 ). The glenohumeral joint has the greatest range of motion of any joint in the human body, and inherently relies upon the rotator cuff to provide dynamic stabilization as well as function to position the arm and hand. Rotator cuff tears are the most common upper extremity con- dition seen by primary care physicians, physiatrists and orthopae- dic surgeons 1 . Rotator cuff injuries present as a spectrum of disease ranging from tendinopathy and partial-thickness tears, extending to full-thickness tears with progressive cartilage degeneration (cuff tear arthropathy) 2  (Box  1  and Fig.  2 ). Symptoms of rotator cuff injuries are broad and can range from minimal symptoms including difficulty sleeping and pain during overhead reaching to loss of active and passive motion with progressive shoulder girdle weakness and dysfunction. Although some tears are traumatic, most rotator cuff problems are degenerative in nature due, in part, to age-related changes to the ­tendon structure. The leading hypothesis is that tears progress from tendinopathic changes in a watershed area that lacks blood supply in the supraspinatus to partial-thickness tears and eventually full- thickness tears 3 , which are accompanied by progressive muscle and tendon degeneration. Confounding the natural history is that not all tears progress and/or worsen, and in many patients who present with a progressive increase in the tear size, symptoms do not worsen, making a standard algorithm for treatment challenging 4 , 5 . The pathophysiology of rotator cuff tears is complex, as it involves the interplay between the tendon, bone and muscle. A key to under - standing rotator cuff pathology and tearing is delineating the role of tendon and muscle degeneration. Increasing evidence suggests that the mTOR pathway might have an important role in the regulation of tendon degeneration over time 6 . Muscle quality, as evidenced by the overall size of the muscle, as well as intramuscular fat deposition (fatty infiltration) also influences shoulder strength, tear progression and the outcome of surgery 7 . Studies also highlight the role of fibroadipo- progenitor cells (FAPs) (muscle-resident multipotent mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs)) as a central mediator of muscle degeneration 8 , 9 . The management of rotator cuff tears is generally based on symptoms. Indeed, most patients with rotator cuff tears diagnosed on advanced imaging (for example, MRI or ultrasonography) do not require surgery. Observation, physical therapy and corticosteroid injections, as well as surgical repair and reconstruction of the rotator cuff, all play a role in patient recovery, which depends on many patient- specific factors such as age, activity level and hand dominance, and intrinsic factors of the rotator cuff. Of note, the terminology of rotator cuff disease in the literature varies: a rotator cuff problem is described as a tendinopathy, a tear, an injury or rotator cuff disease. Thus, the nomenclature leads to ­confusion for the patient and the provider. In this Primer, we present the current knowledge of the basic and clinical science of rotator cuff disease, highlighting the clinical pres- entation, natural history, risk factors and current treatment options.

Patients can develop shoulder pain at any point along the spectrum of rotator cuff tears. Most patients with a rotator cuff tear do not have symptoms of pain or weakness and do not seek medical care. Hence, the exact prevalence of rotator cuff disease is unknown. Although the incidence of symptomatic rotator cuff disease is probably steady, rota- tor cuff repair surgery is more common than 20 years ago, probably due to the increased use of arthroscopic techniques and transition to outpatient surgery. One study showed an annual increase in rota- tor cuff surgery of 1.6% per year in the USA between 2007 and 2016 (165 repairs per 100,000 person-years 10 ). In the absence of pain, indi- viduals with rotator cuff tears have been shown to maintain nearly nor- mal shoulder function 11 . Population screening studies using shoulder ultrasonography have better defined the prevalence of rotator cuff tear 12 . A cross-sectional study involving 1,000 women in the UK demon- strated that 22.2% had a full-thickness rotator cuff tear. Advancing age and dominant shoulder involvement were positively correlated with the presence of a tear. Similarly, two different observational studies in Asian populations showed a prevalence of full-thickness cuff tears of 20.7% (1,366 individuals) and 22.1% (664 individuals), with higher prevalence in individuals ≥57 years of age than in younger individuals and in men than in women 13 , 14 .
Risk factors

section_path: Age .  The overwhelming clinical evidence suggests that the primary
text: Age .  The overwhelming clinical evidence suggests that the primary

risk factor for the development of rotator cuff degeneration is age 4 , 5 , 15 , 16 . Multiple clinical studies have shown that patients with a painful rotator cuff tear often have an asymptomatic degenerative tear in the opposite shoulder 5 , 11 , 17 , 18 . One study performed bilateral shoulder ultrasonog- raphy on patients presenting with unilateral shoulder pain, and dem- onstrated a strong association between cuff disease and ageing 19 . The presence of a full-thickness tear in the painful shoulder was associated with a 21% risk of a partial-thickness tear and 36% risk of a full-thickness tear in the contralateral shoulder. The average age of those with an intact cuff, a unilateral cuff tear and bilateral partial-thickness or full-thickness cuff tears demonstrated a nearly perfect 10-year distri- bution (48.7, 58.7 and 67.8 years of age, respectively) 17 . Patients with a full-thickness cuff tear on the symptomatic side had a 50% chance of having a concomitant cuff tear on the asymptomatic side at ≥66 years of age. One study involving pooled data from 30 separate studies and >6,000 shoulders demonstrated a strong correlation between the pres- ence of a tear and increasing age stratified by decade, with a prevalence of 9.7% in those <20 years of age compared with a prevalence of 62% in those >80 years of age 20 .

Teres minor Infraspinatus stabilize the shoulder against posterior subluxation and is the primary muscle responsible for external rotation of the shoulder. The teres minor lies below the infraspinatus, with a common origin along the medial border of the scapula. It provides up to 45% of the external rotation force. The subscapularis lies on the anterior portion of the scapula and attaches to the lesser tuberosity. Its primary function is internal rotation of the shoulder and it also acts as a force couple (along with the infraspinatus) against the deltoid, keeping the shoulder stabilized within the joint against the pull of the deltoid 302 . The long head of the biceps tendon runs between the supraspinatus and subscapularis in the rotator interval.

The tendons of the teres minor, infraspinatus and supraspinatus are continuous on the humeral head. The supraspinatus muscle lies on the superior portion of the scapula and inserts on the anterior aspect of the greater tuberosity of the humeral head. The primary function of the supraspinatus is forward and lateral elevation of the shoulder, and it assists in rotation of the arm. It is the most commonly torn rotator cuff tendon, involved in 70–85% of rotator cuff tears 13 . The role of scapular morphology, specifically the acromion, in the development of and the progression of degenera- tive rotator cuff tears is debated. For example, in one study, the critical shoulder angle of patients with an asymptomatic rotator cuff tear was larger than in control patients with normal rotator cuffs but was not associated with tear size or the risk of tear enlargement (median time to enlargement of 3.3 years) 51 . Other studies, including a meta-analysis, demonstrated considerable variability in the relationship between criti- cal shoulder angle and the presence of rotator cuff tears, suggesting that it may only be a factor in some patients 52 . The typical site of initiation of a rotator cuff tear is between 13 mm and 17 mm posterior to the biceps tendon within the rotator crescent 16 , 53 . The rotator crescent is a relatively hypovascular area of the posterior cuff that is protected to some degree biomechanically by the thick rotator cable, which spans from the anterior edge of the supraspinatus to the inferior infraspinatus 54 – 56 . Approximately 30% of full-thickness tears, regardless of tear size, involve the anterior cable tissue adjacent to the biceps tendon, and only 3% of small full-thickness tears (≤10 mm) involve this area 53 , 57 . Longitudinal studies have shown that tear enlargement within the rotator crescent is common in the anterior and posterior directions 4 . Rotator cuff tear location mapping has shown that anterior cable disruption is strongly associated with supraspinatus muscle degeneration 16 , 58 . Pain is an important clinical indicator of tear size progression over time with the tear width and amount of retraction from the bone increasing. Much of the research examining tear progression has been performed in asymptomatic shoulders by identifying painless tears in patients presenting with a painful cuff tear in the opposite shoulder. Clinically, most patients in whom the tear progressed had an increased risk of developing pain 59 . In a large study of 224 shoulders in patients with asymptomatic tears in one shoulder and a contralateral painful rotator cuff tear, tear progression was found in 49% of asymptomatic shoulders by ultrasonography, with a median time to enlargement of 2.8 years 5 . In this study, the risk of tear enlargement for full-thickness tears was significantly greater than for partial-thickness tears and control shoulders. Overall, 46% of originally asymptomatic shoulders developed pain at a median of 2.6 years. A subsequent study involv- ing 227 shoulders in patients aged 65 years or younger demonstrated continued tear progression over an average follow up of 7.1 years 43 . Overall, 60% of tears enlarged during the 7-year study period. The risks of enlargement of a full-thickness tear at 2 years, 5 years and 8 years were 26%, 58% and 80%, respectively. Approximately 57% of the cohort developed pain, which was strongly linked to tear enlargement. The presence and progression of degeneration of the supraspinatus and infraspinatus muscles were linked to absolute tear size and tear ­enlargement during follow-up 43 . Clinical evidence strongly suggests that symptomatic degenera- tive full-thickness rotator cuff tears do not heal, and most tears are at risk of slow progression over time. Tear progression can be defined as tear enlargement or progression of muscle degeneration — both of which have implications on healing potential following surgical repair. For degenerative tears, evidence shows that activ- ity tear development is linked to hand dominance, with higher likelihood of a symptomatic tear in the dominant shoulder than the non-dominant shoulder 13 , 41 – 43 . Occupational demands might play a part in rotator cuff tear development; for example, studies have identified several occu- pational exposure risk factors, such as repetitive arm elevation, heavy lifting and use of vibrating tools, that are associated with rotator cuff tears 44 – 48 . However, the exact duration and type of exposure that con- stitute a risk is unknown. A study in a population of >250,000 individu- als (UK Biobank) showed a correlation between self-reported manual labour and the need for rotator cuff surgery compared with ­controls, particularly when the occupational exposure lasted >10 years 49 .
Natural history
Primer

Rotator cuff muscle degeneration refers to the development of muscle atrophy and fatty cell infiltration into the muscle. Rotator cuff muscle degeneration is almost exclusive to full-thickness rotator cuff tears 16 , 57 . In one study, tears that enlarged had more than twice the risk of progression of muscle degeneration than stable tears 61 . Additionally, when tear progression led to disruption of the anterior cable or when the extent of tear enlargement was big, the risk of progression of muscle degeneration increased over time after adjusting for covariates 16 , 61 .

Why a biologically long-standing condition suddenly manifests itself in an acute fashion is still unclear. The present scientific evidence suggests that the initial lesion of tendinopathy is a failed healing response to microtrauma, and the role of inflammation in these conditions has been re-evaluated and is thought to be an important contributor 71 , 72  (Fig.  3 ). In the devel - opment of tendinopathy of the rotator cuff, an increase in inflam- matory cells and production of pro-inflammatory cytokines from tenocytes are thought to have a role 3 , 73 . Animal models of tendino­ pathy show increased numbers of macrophages and mast cells within the injured tendon 74 . In response to altered mechanical load or ­tissue injury, tenocytes and immune cells within the tendon release pro-inflammatory cytokines, including TNF, IL-6 and IL-1β. IL-17A has been found to be upregulated in patients with a rotator cuff tear 75 . IL-17A was expressed in various inflammatory cells within rotator cuff tendon, and tenocytes that were treated with IL-17A had increased production of pro-inflammatory cytokines, and increased expres- sion of apoptosis-related factors 75 . The role of IL-17A is notable given the availability of anti-IL-17A monoclonal antibodies (secukinumab and ixekizumab) that have been evaluated for their role in treating tendinopathy 3 . In most patients, partial-thickness tears and full-thickness tears are hypothesized to be the result of the tendinopathy process over a period of many years. Histologically, tendinopathy demonstrates altered collagen organization with increased inflammatory tissue with decreased vascularity, which, for as-yet unidentified reasons, may not necessarily cause symptoms even as tendinopathy progresses into partial-thickness and full-thickness tears. The severity of tear as well as the extent of tendinopathy do not correlate with patient symptoms, which highlights the complex relationship between degeneration, pain development and pain perception at the central nervous system level. An upregulation of the glutamatergic system has been observed in patients with rotator cuff tendinopathy, with localization of glu- tamate to tenocytes with glutamate receptors on macrophages 76 .
Mechanisms/pathophysiology

Rotator cuff tears represent a spectrum of disease, from impingement, tendinopathy, to partial-thickness tears and to full-thickness tears of varying sizes (Fig.  2 ). Impingement (originally named after the idea that the acromion impinges on the rotator cuff 62 ) occurs when the bursa above the rotator cuff is inflamed as a consequence of altered kinematics of the shoulder girdle. When the larger muscles (deltoid) over-compensate due to rotator cuff dysfunction, the rotator cuff cannot function as an effective force couple, resulting in the humeral head elevating rather than depressing during shoulder motion 63 , 64 . This altered movement pattern increases inflammation within the rotator cuff bursa above the tendon, leading to pain with motion and overhead activity, and pain during sleep. Although imaging findings are normal in patients with mild impingement, increased fluid signal on ultrasonography or MRI above the rotator cuff is a key indication of active impingement. Studies have suggested that the bursa is a source of active healing of the cuff, by stimulating inflammatory cells and fac- tors that promote healing 65 – 67 . The bursal cells display mesenchymal markers of stemness, including CD73 and CD90, and, in experimental models of bursa–tendon crosstalk, the bursa has upregulated markers of extracellular matrix production (TGFβ), immune cell signalling and vascular signalling including PDGFRβ 68 . The exact mechanisms of how rotator cuff injury progresses from tendinopathy to partial-thickness tears and to full-thickness tears is yet to be fully understood. disease and progression

generate reactive oxygen species, which induce inflammation and in excessive quantities, damage cells, lipids, proteins and even DNA 81 , 82 . Clinical studies have shown increased levels of serum hydroperox- ides in tendinopathic tendon tissue and increased concentrations of inflammatory proteins in the synovium and tendon in rotator cuff tears 28 . In animal models, increased mechanical loading and tendon overuse increased the plasma total oxidant status and circulating pro-inflammatory cytokines 79 . Multiple pathways are likely to be involved, and activation of the mTOR pathway 6 , resulting in tissue metaplasia and impaired tenogenesis of tendon-derived stem cells 83 , may have a major role in tendon degeneration and impaired tendon healing. Interestingly, mTOR is also a central regulator of muscle atro- phy; hence, targeting this pathway may serve a dual role in future treatment strategies 84 . Obesity, diabetes mellitus or hypercholesterolaemia further pre- dispose to a pro-inflammatory environment and increase the risk for degenerative tendinopathy. In individuals with comorbidities, reactive oxidative species are increased in these settings, triggering tenocyte apoptosis, fibrosis and tissue metaplasia via mTOR, nicotinamide dinu- cleotide phosphate oxidase (NOX) and AKT/FOXO1 pathways 82 , 85 . Drugs such as fluoroquinolones or local anaesthetics such as bupivacaine also predispose to tendinopathy, probably by altering mitochondrial mem- brane permeability and/or generating reactive oxygen species in cells, thereby inducing oxidative stress and a catabolic, pro-inflammatory response 86 , 87 . Accordingly, anti-oxidants that reduce the production of reactive oxidative species or drugs such as rapamycin, which inhibit down- stream pathways (for example, mTOR), are being actively investi- gated for managing symptomatic tendinopathy 6 . Studies examining natural anti-oxidants such as curcumin, eugenol and flavonoids, are ongoing 80 , 88 . Well-designed clinical studies assessing the efficacy of these therapies in symptomatic patients are warranted.

Interestingly, tenocytes exposed to glutamate in vitro show decreased cell viability and decreased collagen gene expression 77 , providing a possible link between pain and tendon degeneration.
Muscle quality

Muscle quality, defined by the development of muscle atrophy and fatty infiltration, has been implicated as a key factor affecting the outcomes of rotator cuff injury and repair (Fig.  3 ). Muscle atrophy, a likely consequence of mechanical unloading of the tendon and fatty infiltration, which seems to be chronic resulting from alterations in innervation, affects rotator cuff treatment outcomes. Patients with greater muscle atrophy and increased fatty infiltration are at higher risk of retear after surgery, and worse clinical outcomes than those with less muscle atrophy, even with a technically successful repair 7 , 89 .
Tendon degeneration

The cellular and molecular pathophysiology of degenerative tendino­ pathy is highly complex and is only partially understood. A myriad of predisposing factors, including patient-specific factors such as metabolic disorders (that is, hypercholesterolaemia, hyperglycae- mia), mechanical overuse and repetitive microtrauma, drug exposure and ischaemia contribute to profound structural changes within the tendon 3 . Both animal-based and clinical studies have demonstrated that activation of secondary inflammatory pathways and oxidative stress in tendon tissue promote tenocyte apoptosis, fibrosis and disorganization of the extracellular matrix and, in some cases, heterotopic ossification (a condition in which bone tissue develops in soft tissue) 28 , 78 , 79 . section_path: Fatty infiltration .  Fatty infiltration (that is, infiltration of fat from the
text: Fatty infiltration .  Fatty infiltration (that is, infiltration of fat from the

surrounding tissue 102 ) has emerged as an important biological concept involved in muscle degeneration across many conditions, including ageing, low back pain and muscular dystrophy. The presence of fatty infiltration worsens the quality of muscle, as the amount of tissue that can develop force decreases with a concomitant alteration in the biomechanical properties of the tissue 103 . Several animal studies and human studies have demonstrated the importance of FAPs in the development of fatty infiltration 9 . These cells have a central role in the regulation of muscle degeneration; for example, in muscular dystrophy, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis and ageing 104 – 111 . In animal models, FAPs have been shown to proliferate at the time of rotator cuff injury and directly contribute to the development of fatty infil- tration through expression of white fat-associated genes including PPARG ,  CREBP  and other fat-related genes 84 , 96 , 112 . Fatty infiltration can be decreased through inhibition signalling molecules, such as TGFβ, retinoic acid and PPARγ, which probably work through modulating the response of FAPs to injury 113 – 116 . In human studies, FAPs prolifer- ate at the time of rotator cuff injury, and increase in number as tear size progresses 8  and express several fat-related and fibrosis-related genes 117 – 119 . Non-biased transcriptomic studies have shown that FAPs isolated from partial-thickness tears are less likely to express mark- ers of adipogenesis and fibrosis than FAPs from full-thickness tears. Adipogenic commitment is driven by downregulation of DLK1, which is seen in FAPs from patients with full-thickness tears 120 . Importantly, FAPs also seem to have pro-regenerative capabilities in vitro when stimulated with pharmacological agents, which may be utilized to improve muscle quality at the time of and after rotator cuff repair 121 – 124 .
Diagnosis, screening and prevention
Clinical presentation

Patients with a symptomatic rotator cuff tear typically present at ~50–70 years of age, with pain at night that keeps them from sleeping, pain with overhead activity such has reaching a high shelf, or a subjec- tive feeling of shoulder girdle weakness. Despite a clear pathophysi- ological progression from tendinopathy to partial-thickness tears to full-thickness tears, symptom severity does not necessarily correlate with histopathological severity (Fig.  4 ).
Diagnosis

The diagnosis of a rotator cuff tear is based on the location of pain (typically along the lateral shoulder), timing of pain (nocturnal pain and pain with overhead activities) and key physical examination findings. However, the location and type of pain described by patients are often non-specific and only beneficial to a certain extent. Under- standing the symptoms of rotator cuff pathology in comparison with other common shoulder ailments, especially frozen shoulder and gle- nohumeral arthritis, is important. The differential diagnosis of rotator cuff tears is broad and should include degenerative conditions of the shoulder girdle and the cervical spine (Table  1 ). Physical examination of the patients suspected to have rotator cuff pathology should focus on the integrity and functioning of the rotator cuff and periscapular muscles. Despite advances in imaging techniques, history and physical examination remain the primary diagnostic tools and determinants of management.
Tendon healing after repair

Tendon–bone healing, a highly complex process, is crucial to the suc- cess of rotator cuff repair; yet, the mechanism underlying this process remains a critical unmet need in this field. The native enthesis is a highly organized structure with remarkable architecture designed to transmit loads from muscle through unmineralized and mineralized fibrocarti- lage to bone 125 , 126 . Re-establishment of the rotator cuff tendon to bone interface does not recapitulate the organized anatomy or structure of the native enthesis. Lineage tracing studies suggest that even in acute reattachment, the native enthesis is replaced with scar-forming cells leading rather to a mechanically inferior attachment 127 . Healing at the enthesis follows the typical reparative cascade of inflammation, proliferation and remodelling 128 . Pro-inflammatory cytokines released by platelets from the clot recruit macrophages, neutrophils and tendon-derived cells to initiate the repair response. The proliferative phase of collagen and extracellular matrix deposition by recruited fibroblasts results in an increase in cellularity and collagen deposition. During this time, there is also an increase in vascularity. This is followed by remodelling, in which there is a subsequent reduction of cellularity and vascularity that is accompanied by a shift from type III collagen to type I collagen, and morphological reorganization and realignment of the collagen to respond to mechanical loads.

ing both shoulder girdles to assess their symmetry. Muscle atrophy, in the infraspinatus or supraspinatus fossae, is common in patients with chronic, larger rotator cuff tears 134 . The acromioclavicular joint, anterior capsule, coracoid process, posterior capsule, bicipital groove and acromion are palpated for tenderness 135 . Most patients with rotator cuff tears will not report tenderness in these areas, but they should be assessed for concomitant pathology. A comprehensive assessment of shoulder range of motion is per- formed. The cervical spine should be examined to rule out pain coming primarily from a spine condition. Cervical spine pathology typically presents with a history of radiating pain down past the shoulder and into the hand, or associated numbness and tingling in a neurological distribution. During examination of the shoulder, forward flexion, abduction, internal rotation and external rotation are evaluated from the adducted position (that is, with the patient’s arm at the side). Inter- nal and external rotation should also be performed with the shoulder abducted to 90° with the elbow flexed to 90° to check for early arthritis

External rotation strength is tested with the patient’s arm adducted and the elbow flexed to 90°. Weakness with external rotation can be indicative of a tear of the infraspinatus and/or teres minor, which is almost always accompanied by a supraspinatus tear that has extended posteriorly. The patient can also be assessed for a painful arc — the patient must raise the arms above the head and bring the arms down slowly to the sides. In a positive test, the patient reports pain during the 120° movement down to the 40° angle. This test is the most sensitive in patients with a rotator cuff problem; however, the test cannot differentiate between tendinopathy, partial-thickness tear and full-thickness tear 138 .

Several tests are also available to assess the subscapularis muscle and tendon. The shoulder must be placed in an internally rotated position prior to testing to eliminate the strength of the pectoralis. The lift-off test (Gerber test) is performed by placing the arm in an internal rotated position behind the patient’s back 138 . The dorsum of the hand is placed at the base of the lumbar spine and the patient is asked to lift the hand off the back and hold the position. The strength of the manoeuvre is assessed by the examiner by applying counter-pressure to the hand. An inability to lift the hand off the back or inability to resist the examiner’s counter-pressure represents a positive test 140 , 141 . The belly press test evaluates subscapularis function: in a standing or seated position, the patient’s arm is abducted ~20–30° with the arm internally rotated and elbow flexed to 90°. The patient is instructed to press the palm of the hand into the abdomen while keeping the ipsi- lateral elbow in the abducted starting position. The patient not being able to perform the manoeuvre or the elbow falling to the patient’s side during the attempted press indicates a positive test 140 , 142 , 143 . No single test is diagnostically accurate, but the combination of manoeuvres, and correlation with symptoms, facilitates an accurate diagnosis of rotator cuff pathology. However, distinguishing the spec- trum of impingement to partial-thickness to small full-thickness tears remains challenging. Nevertheless, large tears and those with loss of the force couple from tears that extend through the rotator cuff cable 55 are readily diagnosed owing to profound weakness of the shoulder (pseudoparesis) and by a positive external rotation lag sign 138 , 144 .

can aid the examiner in diagnosing rotator cuff pathology. These tests lack high sensitivity and specificity and, therefore, must be used in conjunction with history and imaging to establish an accurate diag- nosis. The Neer test is performed with the examiner standing behind the patient with the affected arm passively elevated with the examiner’s hand stabilizing the scapula. Pain at maximal forward flexion is often indicative of rotator cuff pathology 137 , 139 . In the Hawkins–Kennedy test, the affected arm is passively elevated to 90° of forward flexion and the elbow is placed at 90° of flexion in the neutral position. The forearm is then turned into internal rotation, which brings the greater tuberosity into contact with the undersurface of the acromion. Pain with internal rotation is suggestive of rotator cuff pathology 137 , 140 . The Jobe test is performed to assess the strength of the supraspinatus. The patient places the arm at 90° of abduction and 30° of flexion in the plane of the scapula. Shoulder elevation is manually resisted by the examiner. Pain or weakness denotes a positive test 140 , 141 . Furthermore, the drop arm test can also be used to assess the supraspinatus muscle. The

Advanced MRI techniques, such as IDEAL MRI and Dixon fat– water separation sequences, can allow a more precise measurement of intramuscular fat that allows improved treatment recommendations compared with findings from conventional MRI 89 , 166 – 169 . Information obtained from preoperative MRI can be utilized in scoring systems, such as the rotator cuff healing index, which offer insight into the likelihood of success after surgical treatment 170 . Magnetic resonance arthrography has excellent diagnostic performance and is an additional option for imaging, although the addition of intra-articular contrast material is often not necessary for the routine evaluation of rotator cuff tears 155 , 171 . In areas with limited MRI availability, availability of arthroscopic surgical resources might also be limited, making overall diagnosis and treatment of rotator cuff pathology very challenging.

Radiographs can also effectively diagnose conditions such as greater tuberosity frac - tures, calcific tendinopathy and shoulder osteoarthritis, which may have overlapping symptoms with rotator cuff disease but different treatment pathways 148 , 151 – 153 .
Prevention strategies

When developing prevention strategies for the development of rota- tor cuff tears, it is important to understand that not all risk factors are modifiable. However, strategies should minimize not only the development of rotator cuff pathology but also symptoms. Postural training, flexibility programmes and simple strengthening exercises for the shoulder and scapular muscles can be beneficial in improving shoulder health 179 . These strategies might help to minimize the risk of developing poor posture or form, which can lead to compensation and undue pressure on an ageing rotator cuff, leading to injury. However, studies have not shown a clear link between improving posture and minimizing the incidence of cuff tear and, therefore, the benefits of better posture remain theoretical. Although flexibility and rotator cuff exercises can be beneficial in the prevention of symptoms, studies have shown that excessive repeti- tive use of the rotator cuff can be detrimental 180 . The repetitive nature of overhead sports is a well-known factor that contributes to rota- tor cuff symptoms, although how progression to full-thickness tears occurs is unclear. Limiting the time spent engaged in repetitive exer- cises and activities, careful biomechanical evaluation of performance

Images from MRI can provide an accurate assessment of clinically relevant measurements that help predict the likely success of surgical repair, including tear size 156  and tendon retraction 157 , and depict the pres- ence of associated injuries of the acromioclavicular joint, labrum and glenohumeral cartilage 158 . A fat-sensitive sagittal–oblique sequence is essential for evaluating the rotator cuff muscle quality, which is most commonly performed clinically with the Fuchs modification of the Goutallier classification 159 . The Goutallier classification grades muscle quality from 0 to 4: 0 indicates normal muscle with no fat, 1 indicates streaks of fat in the muscle, 2 indicates more muscle than

Although no clear dietary change can prevent rotator cuff tears, following an anti-inflammatory diet (for example, foods that are high in anti-oxidants such as blueber- ries, almonds and fatty fish) may aid in alleviating pain and inflamma- tion associated with rotator cuff injuries. Further research investigating the role of anti-inflammatory diet is necessary 182 . In animals, vitamin D deficiency may affect the ability of an acutely injured rotator cuff to heal following surgery. Hence, screening for and treating vitamin D deficiency may be beneficial 183 . Evidence shows a possibility of longer duration of symptoms and a worse prognosis in patients with depres- sion and anxiety than in those without. Patients should be screened to identify those with these common pathologies so that they can be counselled to improve outcomes 184 .

Medical management

Pharmacotherapy, including medications such as acetaminophen, NSAIDs and corticosteroids, can be used for short-term pain relief in patients with symptomatic rotator cuff tears. If no contraindications exist, acetaminophen is commonly used as the first-line medication and NSAIDs are suggested for intermittent use as a second-line treatment. Oral and topical NSAIDs can effectively relieve shoulder pain secondary to tear if used for a short time period (4–8 weeks) 196 , 197  and have been shown to be safe preoperatively and postoperatively 198 . The adverse effects of these medications should be taken into consideration when used as long-term treatment. Subacromial corticosteroid injections (CSIs) are recommended in the inflammatory stage or early stages of rotator cuff tears to reduce pain and improve function in patients with moderate-to- severe pain and in those who failed initial physical therapy and/or oral anti-inflammatory drugs. CSIs can be effective in the short term, especially for small tears, and CSIs in combination with physical therapy can successfully reduce pain and improve function within 6 weeks. This combination can allow some patients to avoid surgery altogether. If not successful after 6 weeks of treatment, the long-term efficacy of CSIs is limited. Importantly, preoperative CSIs may be associated with increased rates of postoperative infections and revision surgery if administered within 1 month of surgery 199 – 201 . Furthermore, the admin- istration of two or more CSIs the year before surgery substantially increases the risk of subsequent revision rotator cuff surgery 199 .
Management

The goals of management are to reduce pain and enable return to function, including improved sleep. In asymptomatic patients or in those with minimal shoulder symptoms, observation alone might be sufficient. These patients should be made well aware of the risks of progression, with pain serving as a key marker of likely tear ­progression over time and an indicator warranting evaluation and treatment.
Physical therapy

The predominant treatment modality in patients with symptomatic rota- tor cuff tendinopathy, or partial-thickness or full-thickness tears is phys- ical therapy. Physical therapy is effective and when successful, results in quicker return to activities and function than surgical treatment 185 . The MOON Shoulder Group followed 381 patients (31–90 years of age; mean age 62 years) with atraumatic full-thickness rotator cuff tears over 2 years, and demonstrated substantial improvements in patient-reported outcomes (PROs) including American Shoulder and Elbow Surgeons (ASES) scores and a reduced rate of surgery after physical therapy 186 . Patients undertook 6–12 weeks of a standardized supervised physical therapy programme focusing on basic rotator cuff strengthening and shoulder mobility, and >70% of the patients demon- strated improvements in PROs and were satisfied with non-operative management. Importantly, belief that physical therapy would improve their symptoms was the most important factor in predicting the suc- cess of therapy 187 . Several other randomized studies have shown similar outcomes comparing physical therapy and surgical intervention with short-term and mid-term follow-up 188 , 189 , although long-term PROs may favour surgery in some patients at 10 years after presentation 190 . Even in the setting of massive tears, a deltoid muscle rehabilitation regimen is effective in improving function and pain 191 . The goal of physical therapy is restoration of full, pain-free range of motion, flexibility, muscle balance and scapulothoracic and glenohumeral muscular control and stability. Improving scapular stability, proper neuromuscular control of the shoulder girdle and thoracic posture are essential in a well-designed rotator cuff exercise programme 192 , 193 . Potential contributing mechanisms to abnormal scapular kinematics include pain, soft-tissue tightness, altered muscle activation or strength imbalances, muscle fatigue and poor thoracic posture 194 , 195 . Platelet-rich plasma

Many studies have investigated platelet-rich plasma (PRP) as a potential non-operative treatment for rotator cuff tears and have shown mixed results regarding the effectiveness of these injections. The studies showed reduced pain and improved function in patients with tendi- nopathy at 6 months with deterioration to baseline levels by 1 year 202 . A meta-analysis in 2023 demonstrated that PRP was equivalent to CSIs in terms of pain relief and function at any given time point 203 . In addi- tion, these studies showed substantial variability in the number of PRP injections, injection volume and platelet concentration. According to the 2019 clinical practice guidelines from the American Academy of Orthopaedic Surgeons, evidence is limited to support the routine use of PRP for treating cuff tendinopathy or partial-thickness tears and, currently, PRP is not indicated for the treatment of full-thickness tears.
Mesenchymal stem cells

The advent of MSCs has opened new potential avenues for therapeutic interventions for rotator cuff tears. This technique involves the injec- tion of MSCs into the rotator cuff tendon or into the subacromial bursa. Studies have shown significant improvements in pain and functional outcomes in patients treated with MSCs compared with placebo, but further research assessing mid-term and long-term outcomes is needed to standardize their use 204 . Moreover, the use of culture-expanded MSCs is not approved by the FDA and, therefore, these cellular treat- ments are only available in the USA within an Investigational New Drug

Although the biomechanical properties and retear rates of double-row suture bridge repair are better than simple single-row repair 229 – 231 , clinical outcomes according to repair technique are not significantly different 232 . The use of orthobiologics, such as MSCs intraoperatively has shown promising results to decrease retear rates 233 . In a prospective study 234 , bone marrow aspirate concentrate augmentation resulted in a 39% decrease in retear rate at 1 year compared with a control group who underwent rotator cuff repair only. For PRP, several studies have shown improved healing rates, particularly in larger tears, but clinical outcomes are not improved by PRP augmentation 235 – 238 . Although PRP may enable some early mobilization and pain relief, the long-term outcomes are comparable to those following well-performed rota- tor cuff repair 239 , 240 . The clinical evidence for the use of PRP in the non-operative management of rotator cuff disorders is inconsistent, making inferences difficult. Furthermore, in one study in patients with rotator cuff tears managed with arthroscopic surgical repair, there was no difference in clinical and imaging outcomes at 10 years follow-up between those receiving and those not receiving PRP injections 241 . The wide range of effectiveness can be related to the types of PRP used and the variability in preparation techniques, cell counts and growth factors available 242 . These differences make it necessary to plan adequately powered appropriately standardized studies with clinically relevant outcome measures. In patients with an irreparable tear, when retracted tendons can- not be re-attached to the greater or lesser tuberosity without arthritic change of the glenohumeral joint, other treatment options are con- sidered, especially in young, active patients. These include partial repair, superior capsular reconstruction (reconstructing the superior capsule using allograft or autogenic tensor fascia lata), an allogenic or xenogenic patch bridging graft, muscle advancement (releasing the proximal rotator cuff muscle to re-attach to the footprint), tendon transfer, subacromial balloon spacer, and other procedures 243 – 246 . Results with these diverse treatment strategies are mixed, as expected in small case series with complex conditions. If the rotator cuff tear is assumed to be irreparable in older patients and/or if accompanied by arthritic change of the gleno- humeral joint, reverse shoulder replacement is the primary treatment option 247 . Reverse shoulder arthroplasty reverses normal anatomy, so that the glenoid has the ‘ball’ side (glenosphere on the glenoid side) articulating with the humeral cup (metal tray with polyethylene insert). This technique has excellent long-term outcomes for irreparable cuff tears and cuff tear arthropathy 247 , 248 . The 10-year success rate of reverse shoulder arthroplasty is up to 93%, but functional outcomes deterio- rate over time, particularly in patients >80 years of age 249 – 251 . Various complications related to reverse shoulder including scapular acro- mial notching, instability, infection and glenoid loosening can occur (at rates of 1–5%) 252 – 257 . An overall algorithm for treatment is presented in Fig.  5 . Rotator cuff repairs with concomitant moderate-to-severe glenohumeral osteo- arthritis had worse outcomes than rotator cuff tears with mild gleno- humeral osteoarthritis 258 . Surgical intervention

Subacromial decompression including acromioplasty is a widely per- formed procedure to treat subacromial impingement syndrome 205 , 206 . Despite the theoretical advantages of acromioplasty, its role in pain management and/or prevention of progression to rotator cuff tear is still a matter of debate. Several randomized trials found superiority of acro - mioplasty to exercise therapy alone 207 , 208 ; however, long-term follow-up studies found no significant differences in ­clinical outcomes 209 – 213 . All-arthroscopic rotator cuff repair is considered the treatment of choice for rotator cuff tears that fail non-operative management or for acute traumatic rotator cuff tears. However, retear remains an unsolved problem, reported for 10–40% of procedures 214 . Short-term outcomes are favourable regardless of retear; however, in many patients with retear, long-term outcomes are worse than in patients without retear and deteriorate over time 215 – 217 . To prevent retear, accurate prediction of reparability and healing after repair is important. Predictors of retear can be classified as tear characteristic factors and patient demographic factors. Tear charac- teristic factors include tear size 170 , 218 – 220 , fatty infiltration and muscle atrophy 221 . These factors could decrease the elasticity and vitality of the rotator cuff tendon tissues, inhibiting healing after rotator cuff repair 221 , 222 . Old age, diabetes, osteoporosis, dyslipidaemia, obesity and smoking are demographic risk factor for retear 170 , 218 , 222 – 225 . Using a 15-point scoring system to predict healing after cuff repair 170  (Table  2 ), retear rate in patients with ≤4 points was 6.0%; however, rates in those with ≥5 and ≥10 points were 55.2% and 86.2%, respectively. Given the high retear rates, there has been considerable focus on improving the biomechanical and biological construct of the repair. The ideal biomechanical suture configuration for rotator cuff repair requires mechanical stability until tendon-to-bone healing occurs, without excessive tension, which can damage the repaired tendon 226 , 227 . section_path: Non-surgical management .  Relatively few studies have investigated
text: Non-surgical management .  Relatively few studies have investigated

the long-term quality of life and overall outcomes in patients treated non-operatively for full-thickness rotator cuff tear. For example, in one study that followed 93 patients for five or more years, 25 patients (27%) had undergone surgery at the latest follow-up. Of 63 patients who completed the rotator cuff quality of life (RC-QOL) questionnaire, those treated non-operatively scored 83 out of 100 points and those treated surgically scored 89 out of 100 (ref.  270 ). Isolated full-thickness supraspinatus tears seem to respond well to non-operative manage- ment in the long term, with reasonable overall outcomes and lim- ited progression of fatty infltration 271 . Tear progression seems to be associated with recurrent or deteriorating symptoms and, therefore, some patients who initially respond well to non-surgical treatment may undergo surgery in the future. In a long-term randomized con- trolled trial, the non-operative group did not do as well as the surgically repaired group at 10 years, suggesting that, in younger or more active patients, repair may be preferred 190 , a concept that was supported in a propensity-matched study 272 .
Quality of life

Rotator cuff tears of all sizes and cuff tear arthropathy can be painful with the potential to compromise motion, strength and function 261 , 262 . By contrast, structural change to the rotator cuff can be identified in a large proportion of individuals (~60 years of age) who never complain of shoulder pain or loss of function 263 . Hence, the effect of rotator cuff disease on well-being remains difficult to analyse. Psychosocial and mental health problems are associated with greater symptoms and worse function in patients with symptomatic rotator cuff disease than in patients without mental health issues 33 , 264 , 265 . Whether the rotator cuff disease is further disabling because of worse mental coping mechanisms or if the chronicity of rotator cuff disease leads to psychological and mental dysfunction in these individuals remains unclear. MSCs and bone marrow aspirate concentrate, to improve tendon–bone healing and tendinopathy. Preliminary findings from experimental models and clinical studies are encouraging but are in the early stages of research 281 , 282 . A population of MSCs reside in the subacromial bursa, which are responsive to inflammatory stimuli, indicating an as-yet-unexplored biological role in the context of rotator cuff disease 283 . Bursa–tendon crosstalk has an interesting clinical relevance and could be a novel target in the management of shoulder disorders. Indeed, using a rat model of rotator cuff injury and repair, proteomic profiling of the bursa demonstrated increased cytokine expression and MSC pro- liferation after rotator cuff tears, probably to protect the adjacent intact tissue and to maintain the morphology of the underlying bone 65 . The bursa, therefore, may have an initiating and essential role in heal- ing by acting as a potential cellular source to promote healing after repair 81 . Collagen injections have been used to treat rotator cuff tendi- nopathy and to support arthroscopic repair. Their mechanism of action is not completely understood, but they have been hypothesized to induce regenerative pathways, to stimulate tenocyte proliferation and migration, and to increase endogenous collagen synthesis to restore collagen fibres in damaged tendons 284 – 287 . Integrin receptors in fibro- blasts are activated and, consequently, the growth factor cascade initiates the synthesis of endogenous collagen, healing the damaged collagen fibres and leading to proper alignment 288 – 292 . Despite the use of different types of collagens and injection protocols, or applying different PROs, studies have shown clinically and functionally relevant improvements. Novel surgical procedures to improve outcomes of rotator cuff repair are consistently being attempted, but management of large and massive tears remains a considerable challenge. In patients with massive rotator cuff tears (>5 cm), repair of the superior capsule as well as the rotator cuff has been reported to improve retear rates at early follow up 293 , 294 . The superior capsule defect has been defined as the essential lesion of rotator cuff tears 295 , and several researchers recom- mend its repair to restore shoulder biomechanics, preventing the rise of the humeral head in the postoperative period 296 , 297 . It is also thought to provide an additional source of autologous tenocytes, which may improve the biology of the repair. The long head of the biceps tendon, that is rich in tenocytes and fibroblasts, is a major stabilizer of the humeral head preventing supe- rior migration and reinforcing repair 298 – 300 . Its use for cuff repair may provide a local autologous source of collagen, and can be incorporated in the repair arthroscopically with no further surgical exposure or donor site morbidity 298 . Optimal management of shoulder disability may be influenced by the more detailed understanding of the natural history of disease and the anticipated operative outcomes. Machine learning employs computer algorithms and statistical analysis to determine complex trends and patterns, which can be difficult to perform in a clinical set- ting. Studies have shown promising results in accurately predicting postoperative scores. This methodology may be improved to support preoperative counselling, planning and resource allocation 301 . Overall, rotator cuff pathology is a substantial clinical problem with room for improvements in our understanding of pathophysiology,

vides substantial improvements in pain, function and quality of life in many individuals with an irreparable cuff tear, particularly cuff tear arthropathy 278 , 279 . Reverse shoulder arthroplasty is particularly useful when the subscapularis and the posterosuperior cuff are irreparable, as well as in the presence of associated arthritis. However, a subset of patients — individuals with an irreparable cuff tear, no arthritis and good preoperative active motion — are particularly at risk of ­dissatisfaction after reverse arthroplasty 280 .
